event: 29-year-old
Woman
Self-inflicted gunshot injury to the abdomen
Three wounds involved the epigastrium
Three wounds involved the left hypochondrium
Three wounds involved the left lumbar area
Underwent laparotomy at another hospital
Perforation in the antrum of the stomach
Multiple small bowel perforations
Primary surgical repair of stomach and small bowel perforations
Bilateral lateral (zone 2) retroperitoneal hematomas
Retroperitoneal hematomas were non-expanding and non-pulsatile
Retroperitoneal hematomas were initially not explored
Transferred to the intensive care unit (ICU)
Developed pulmonary embolism (PE)
Developed disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
Managed with blood products
Recovered over a period of one month
Discharged home in a stable condition
Presented to a local health center with left iliac fossa (LIF) pain
Presented with watery stool
Reduced hemoglobin level
Raised serum C-reactive protein (CRP)
Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT scan showed large left-sided retroperitoneal collection
CT showed contrast extravasation into the retroperitoneal space
Indicating left ureteral injury with urinoma formation
Indicating colonic fistula
Referred to our trauma center
Underwent cystoscopy with a retrograde pyelogram
Showed complete transection of the left upper ureter
Contrast extravasation into the retroperitoneal space
Underwent left percutaneous nephrostomy
Left-sided pigtail drain was inserted into the urinoma
Treated with antibiotics throughout hospital admission
Sepsis workup investigations showed presence of a multi-drug-resistant coliform
Clear urine was draining from the urinoma during hospital stay
Repeat CT scan one week later showed reduction in urinoma size
Further well-defined collection in right side of pelvis compressing urinary bladder
Underwent another pigtail drain insertion
Drain removed after 48 hours
Discharged in a stable condition
Brought back to hospital three weeks later for elective surgery
Cystoscopy prior to surgery was normal apart from extra-luminal compression of the bladder
Intraoperatively, 2.5 cm segment of dense fibrosis seen in the upper ureter
Left end-to-end ureteric anastomosis (ureteroureterostomy) was performed
Anastomosis performed over a double J stent (DJS)
Colonoscopy done intra-operatively
Methylene blue testing was negative suggesting spontaneous fistula healing
Discharged from hospital on the third post-operative day
Seen in outpatient department two months later
Flexible cystoscopy with removal of the left DJS
